1: Standard word-event formula opens the two-part oracle against the prince of Tyre (vv1-10) and the king of Tyre (vv11-19). The distinction between nagid (prince/leader) and melek (king) may reflect political reality — Ithobalos II of Tyre used the title nagid — or may be a deliberate prophetic choice to deny Tyre's ruler the full royal dignity.
2: ben-ʾadam ʾemor lenagid Tsur koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH yaʿan gabaḥ libbekha vattoʾmer ʾel ʾani moʾshav ʾelohim yashavti belev yammim — the first sin named: gabaḥ libbekha (your heart was lifted up/proud). The prince's claim is ʾel ʾani (I am a god) — not merely king-like or divinely appointed, but divine identity claimed as his own. The phrase moʾshav ʾelohim (seat/dwelling of God) locates the claim geographically: the prince sits in the divine dwelling-place (the sea-citadel of Tyre = the mythological sea-abode of the divine assembly). The contrast: veʾattah ʾadam velo-ʾel — "but you are a man and not God."
3: ḥakham ʾattah miDaniʾel kol-setum loʾ ʿamam ʿaddekha — "you are wiser than Daniel; no secret is hidden from you." The ironic comparison to Daniel — almost certainly the legendary Daniʾel of ANE wisdom tradition (not the biblical Daniel, whose book was written later) — is the same figure referenced in 14:14, 20. Tyre's king claims a wisdom (ḥakham) surpassing this proverbially wise man. The pride is compounded: he claims both divine identity and surpassing wisdom.
4: The wisdom used for commercial gain — bekhokhmateikha uvivunateikha ʿasita lekha ḥayil — "by your wisdom and your understanding you have made wealth for yourself" — the connection between ḥokhmah (wisdom) and commercial success is a theme of Proverbs (8:18-19; 3:16). What distinguishes Tyre's case is that the wisdom-wealth is self-generated and self-attributed.
5: The causal chain — wisdom → gold → pride: berov ḥokhmateikha birekhulatekha hirbeita ḥeileikh vayyigbaḥ levaveikha beḥeileikha — "by your great wisdom in your trade you have increased your wealth and your heart has become proud because of your wealth." The wayhi-consecutive (vayyigbaḥ levaveikha) marks the pride as a consequence of the prosperity.
6: The divine response to the divine-claim — lakhein koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH yaʿan sitteikha libbekha klevav ʾelohim — "therefore thus says Lord YHWH: because you have compared your heart to the heart of God" — the verb shat (set/compare) in sitteikha libbekha (you have set/compared your heart) uses the same root as ʾani YHWH sham (I YHWH have set it there) in other oracular contexts. The setting/comparing of the heart to the divine heart is the specific theological offense.
7: veheveʾeti ʿaleikha zarim ʿaritsim goyim — "therefore I am about to bring strangers against you, the most ruthless of nations." Aritsim (ruthless/terrible ones) from arits (to be ruthless, to cause fear) — the same word used for the Babylonians in Ps 54:3 and Isa 29:5. YHWH deploying the most ruthless nations against the self-styled divine ruler is the counter-force.
8: Descent to the pit — lashaḥat yoridukha vamata ḥallelei ḥalal belev yammim — "they will thrust you down into the pit and you will die the death of those who are slain in the heart of the seas." The irony: the divine dwelling in the heart of the sea (belev yammim in v2) becomes the death-location in v8. The claim to inhabit the divine seat is overturned by dying in the same sea.
9: The deathbed non-deity — haʾamor toʾmar ʾel ʾani lifnei horegekha veʾattah ʾadam velo-ʾel beyad meḥallelekha — "will you still say 'I am God' in the presence of the one who slays you? You are a man and not God in the hand of the one who wounds you." The repetition of ʾadam velo-ʾel (a man and not God) from v2 is the literary inclusio of the first oracle.
10: mot ʿarelim tamut beyad zarim — "you will die the death of the uncircumcised by the hand of strangers." Death by the hand of the uncircumcised (ʿarelim) is the most dishonorable death for a Semitic ruler. The term ʿarel (uncircumcised) carries the full weight of covenant-outside status — to die at the hands of those outside the covenant community is to die without honor, the antithesis of the divine status the prince claimed.
11: The second oracle — seʾa qinah ʾal-melek Tsur — shifts the address from nagid (prince) to melek (king) and changes the genre from accusation-oracle to qinah (lament). The shift in title has been interpreted as: (1) a second historical ruler; (2) a shift from the human ruler to his demonic counterpart; (3) a rhetorical elevation of the addressee to heighten the contrast of fall. The lament form applies the highest possible status claims to Tyre's ruler precisely to make the fall most devastating.
12: attah ḥotem toknit male ḥokhmah vekhilil yofi — "you were the seal of perfection, full of wisdom and perfect in beauty." Ḥotem toknit (seal/pattern of perfection) — toknit from takan (to measure/construct, be established); the "seal of the measuring pattern" describes the king as the perfect exemplar of what a being should be. The same cluster of terms (ḥokhmah, yofi) appears in ch 27's description of Tyre-the-ship.
13: The Eden setting — beʿEden gan-haʾElohim hayita — "you were in Eden, the garden of God." The twelve precious stones that covered him (even yequrah mekhaseka) correspond approximately to the twelve stones of the high priest's breastplate (Exod 28:17-20), though the lists differ and the text is partially corrupt in both Hebrew and LXX traditions. The king-in-Eden parallel to Adam is the template for the next verses.
14: ʾatt-kerub mimshakh hasokek venatatikha — "you were an anointed guardian cherub, and I placed you." Kerub mimshakh (anointed cherub) — the mimshakh (anointed/spread out) could modify either the cherub (as anointed) or the position (as covering/spanning); the LXX renders it differently. Hasokek (covering/screening) from sakhakh (to cover, used of the cherubim over the ark in Exod 25:20) — the covering function identifies this cherub with the role of the ark-cherubim. The holy mountain of God (har qodesh ʾElohim) is a mythological term for the divine assembly's location (also Isa 14:13; Ps 48:2).
15: tamim ʾattah biderakhekha miyom hibborekha ʿad-nimtsaʾ ʿavelah vakh — "you were blameless in your ways from the day you were created until unrighteousness was found in you." The term tamim (blameless, complete) is the character-descriptor of Noah (Gen 6:9) and Abraham (Gen 17:1), and Job (Job 1:1). Its use here establishes that the king's primordial state was genuine moral integrity, not just surface perfection — making the subsequent fall all the more tragic.
16: The sin that caused the fall — berob rekhullatekha mallu tokhekha ḥamas vatetaʾ — "in the abundance of your trade you were filled with violence and you sinned." The trade-pride (rekhullah) that filled him with hamas (violence/injustice) is the commercial corruption that corrupted the primordial wisdom. The verb vatteḥeʾ (and you sinned, qal imperfect consecutive) narrates the moral causation simply and directly.
17: The pride of beauty — gabaḥ libbekha beyofeikh shihhateta ḥokhmateikha ʿal-yifʾeikha — "your heart was proud because of your beauty; you corrupted your wisdom for the sake of your splendor." The three corruptions: pride (gabaḥ) because of beauty (yofi), corruption (shihhat) of wisdom because of splendor (yifʾeikha). The sequence — wisdom → beauty → pride → corruption — is Tyre's tragedy in compressed form.
18: The profaning of the sanctuaries — merov ʿavoneikha beʿevel rekhullatekha ḥillalta miqdashekha — "by the multitude of your iniquities, in the unrighteousness of your trade, you profaned your sanctuaries." Ḥillel (piel: profane/defile) applied to one's own sanctuaries (miqdashekha) — the commercial corruption extended into the sacred sphere. YHWH's response: vaʾotsaʾ ʾesh mittokekha — "I brought forth fire from your midst" — the fire from within Tyre itself as the agent of self-destruction.
19: The universal horror conclusion — kol yodeʿeikh baʿammim shamemuʾ ʿaleikha ballahot hayita veʾeynnekha leʿolam ʾad — "all who know you among the peoples are appalled at you; you have become a terror and you shall be no more forever." The ballahot (terrors) conclusion is the third time this word closes an Ezekiel oracle in chs 26-28 (also 26:21; 27:36), creating a formal refrain of terror and non-existence across the anti-Tyre oracle complex.
20: Standard word-event formula opens the brief oracle against Sidon (vv20-26). Sidon was Tyre's neighbor and the older Phoenician city; the pair Tyre-and-Sidon appears frequently in prophetic literature (Isa 23; Jer 27:3; Joel 3:4).
21: The address to Sidon — sim panekha ʾel-Tsidon vehinave ʿaleha — uses the set-face formula (sim panekha ʾel) that Ezekiel uses for Jerusalem (4:3), the mountains of Israel (6:2), the south (20:46), and Ammon (25:2). The formula marks the prophetic oracle as a directed, intentional divine word.
22: YHWH's hostile-presence formula — hinneni ʿaleikh Tsidon venikkabdeti betokhekh — "I am against you, Sidon, and I will be glorified in your midst." The divine self-glorification (nikkabed, niphal of kavad, be honored/glorified) through judgment is a recurring theme: divine honor is established when divine words are vindicated by events. The recognition formula — veyadeʿu ki ʾani YHWH — follows immediately.
23: Plague and sword in Sidon's streets — veshillaḥti vah dever vedam beḥutsoteha venafall ḥalal betokhah baḥerev ʿaleha missaviv — the sword-from-outside and plague-from-within complement each other as siege-weapons. The encircling sword (ʿaleha missaviv) is standard siege-attack language.
24: The removal of the thorn from Israel's side — veloʾ yihyeh ʿod lebeit Yisraʾel sillon mamʾir vequts makʾiv mikkol sevivotam habbazim ʾotam veyadeʿu ki ʾani ʾAdanai YHWH — "and for the house of Israel there shall be no more a brier to prick or a thorn to cause pain from all their neighbors who have treated them with contempt." Sillon mamʾir (pricking brier) and quts makʾiv (painful thorn) are the images for the surrounding hostile nations that perpetually irritate/wound Israel. Their removal is the positive aspect of the OAN judgments.
25: The gathering and restoration oracle — koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH beqabbetstsi ʾet-beit Yisraʾel min-haʿammim ʾasher nafotsu vam veniqddashti vam leʾeinei haggoyim — "when I gather the house of Israel from the peoples among whom they are scattered, I will manifest my holiness in them before the eyes of the nations." The gathering-from-the-nations promise echoes 11:17 and 20:34. Veniqddashti vam (I will be sanctified among them) — divine sanctification through the gathered community: the nations' acknowledgment of YHWH comes through his restoration of Israel.
26: The security of dwelling in the land — veyashvu ʿaleha lavetaḥ uvanu batim venataʿu keramim veyashvu lavetaḥ beʿasoti shefatim bekol-hazim otam missevivotam veyadeʿu ki ʾani YHWH ʾeloheyhem — "they will dwell securely, building houses and planting vineyards; they will dwell securely when I execute judgments against all their neighbors who have treated them with contempt, and they will know that I am YHWH their God." The covenant-security formula (yashav lavetaḥ, dwell in safety; benot batim, build houses; nataʿ keramim, plant vineyards) is the Deuteronomic covenant-blessing formula. The final veyadeʿu ki ʾani YHWH ʾeloheyhem (they will know that I am YHWH their God) is the only instance in the OAN section where the recognition formula includes ʾeloheyhem (their God) — marking the Sidon oracle as the passage back toward Israel's restoration.
